content:
First, rephrase the “no‐adjacency” condition more graph‐theoretically.  Think of the 1000×1000 board as the grid‐graph G with 10^6 vertices.  A domino is an edge in G, and the condition “no two dominoes are adjacent” means that no two chosen edges share a vertex or even have their endpoints joined by another edge.  In graph theory this is called an induced matching.  

1) Upper bound by a counting–forbidding argument.  
   If you place one domino (an edge uv) then the two vertices u,v are occupied, and each of u and v has up to 3 other neighbors (besides each other) which are now forbidden to be used by any other domino.  That knocks out 2 occupied vertices + 6 forbidden = 8 vertices per domino.  Since there are 10^6 total vertices,  
     maximum number of dominoes ≤ 10^6 / 8 = 125 000.  

2) Matching lower bound by a 4×4–block construction.  
   Tile the 1000×1000 board by 4×4 blocks.  In each 4×4 block place exactly two non‐adjacent dominoes, for instance (in local block coordinates 1≤i,j≤4) place  
     • one horizontal domino on cells (2,2)–(2,3),  
     • one vertical domino on cells (3,1)–(4,1).  
   One checks that within a block these two don’t touch, and that when you repeat the same pattern in every 4×4 block, no domino in one block becomes adjacent to any domino in a neighboring block.  

Since there are (1000/4)^2=250^2=62 500 blocks and each block carries 2 dominoes, this achieves 2⋅62 500=125 000 dominoes.  

Combined with the 10^6/8 upper bound, we conclude the maximum is exactly
\boxed{125000}.
